title: Variables: val vs var
description: Learn how to store data in memory.

slides.title: What is a Variable? 📦
slides.content:
## Storage Boxes for Data

Imagine a variable as a labeled box inside the computer's memory. You put a piece of data inside it, and whenever you need that data later, you simply call out the label on the box.

slides.title: The Two Types of Boxes ⚖️
slides.content:
In Kotlin, variables come in two flavors:

1. **`val` (Value)**: A read-only variable. Once you put something inside, you can **never change** it. Like a permanent marker.
2. **`var` (Variable)**: A mutable variable. You can change the contents inside the box whenever you want. Like a pencil and an eraser.

quizData.question: Which keyword is used to declare a variable whose value cannot be changed later?
quizData.explanation: A 'val' (short for value) is a read-only, immutable variable.

slides.title: Creating Variables 🛠️
slides.content: Here is how you define both types. Notice we use the `=` sign to assign a value to the label.
slides.codeSnippet:
val name = "Alex"
var age = 20

println(name)
println(age)

slides.title: Changing Values 🔄
slides.content: Let's try to update the values. A year passes, so Alex is now 21.
slides.codeSnippet:
var age = 20
age = 21 // This works perfectly!
println(age)

val name = "Alex"
// name = "Bob" // ❌ This would cause a massive error!

quizData.question: What happens if you try to reassign a new value to a variable defined with `val`?
quizData.explanation: Variables declared with `val` are strictly read-only. The compiler will give you an error if you try to reassign them.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Best Practice

Always use `val` by default! Only change it to `var` when you are absolutely sure the value needs to be updated later. This makes your code safer and reduces bugs.
